output: Batch 3 executed as a verification-only step. No source changes were required: `computeDropPosition` already emits the same `isNewTrack` signal for multi-drag, `DropIndicator` already shows the new-track affordance when `trackId` is undefined, and secondary ghosts remain visible near edges by rendering relative to the source row when there is not yet a concrete target row. Verification passed and the batch checkpoint was written to `.megaplan/plans/multi-clip-drag-should-20260406-0421/execution_batch_3.json`.

task_updates.executor_notes: Verified the near-edge multi-drag behavior without a code patch. `computeDropPosition` derives `isNewTrack` from row geometry and source kind rather than selection size, so multi-clip drags get the same new-track state as single-clip drags. `DropIndicator` already shows the affordance when `position.isNewTrack === true && position.trackId === undefined`. `useClipDrag` still falls back to `session.sourceRowId` for secondary ghost placement when no real target row exists, and `computeSecondaryGhosts` therefore keeps secondary ghosts visible relative to that source row. This matches the plan’s acceptable FLAG-002 behavior, so the optional virtual-row polish was not needed.

sense_check_acknowledgments.executor_note: Confirmed by inspection that the DropIndicator affordance is driven by `isNewTrack && trackId === undefined`, which `computeDropPosition` already produces for multi-drag and `useDragCoordinator` already forwards unchanged. Confirmed that secondary ghosts remain visible near edges because `useClipDrag` falls back to `session.sourceRowId`, so `computeSecondaryGhosts` renders them against a real source row instead of disappearing.
